channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump’s Threats to Europe Put Its Leaders in a Double Bind Over Iran
item.description: European politicians risk angering their voters if they join America’s war. Yet they could also face domestic upheaval if they take no action to reopen shipping routes that Iran has blocked and ease an energy crisis.
item.dc:creator: Mark Landler
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Donald Trump disembarking from Air Force One in Maryland on Monday.

item.title: Asia Is Getting Crushed Between Oil Prices and the Dollar
item.description: From India to Southeast Asia to South Korea, currencies are crumbling as governments race to secure fuel that is priced in American money.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Choe Sang-Hun, Sui-Lee Wee and Jason Gutierrez
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Shammi Mehra/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: People lining up to refuel at a gas station in Ahmedabad in India on Monday.

item.title: Trump Had His Eye on China, Then Plunged Into a New Mideast War
item.description: The Trump administration said the Middle East would “recede” in importance as the China challenge took priority. But the president started the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Maxim Shemetov/Reuters
item.media:description: A military parade in Beijing last year. Three American presidents have entered office vowing to focus resources on the challenges posed by China. Each instead turned to crises in other parts of the world.

item.title: Meta and YouTube Found Negligent in Landmark Social Media Addiction Trial
item.description: A jury found the companies harmed a young user with design features that were addictive and led to her mental health distress.
item.dc:creator: Cecilia Kang, Ryan Mac and Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Mark Abramson for The New York Times
item.media:description: Meta’s chairman and chief executive, Mark Zuckerberg, at Los Angeles Superior Court last month.

item.title: Meta Lays Off 700 Employees, While Rewarding Top Executives
item.description: The jobs cuts and a new stock program for executives come as Meta continues to shift its focus to artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Eli Tan
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times

item.title: ICE Agents at Some Airports Begin Checking IDs in Security Lines
item.description: It was unclear whether having agents helping with screening passengers would improve wait times. Some travelers expressed worries.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales, Christine Chung, Hamed Aleaziz, Sean Keenan and Rowan Moore Gerety
item.media:credit: Megan Varner/Getty Images
item.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents at Hartsfield-Jackson International Airport in Atlanta on Wednesday.

item.title: Homeland Security Talks Hit Snag as Democrats Demand ICE Restrictions
item.description: A day after Republicans offered to remove money for ICE enforcement from a Department of Homeland Security funding bill, Democrats insisted that the deal must include curbs on federal agents.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer said a Democratic counteroffer would “rein in” Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents “with common-sense guardrails.”

item.title: Why Are Delays at Houston’s Bush Airport Worse Than at Others?
item.description: Lines were long at airports nationwide Tuesday amid T.S.A. staffing shortages, but passengers at Bush Intercontinental Airport waited more than four hours to pass through checkpoints.
item.dc:creator: Christina Morales
item.media:credit: Antranik Tavitian/Getty Images
item.media:description: The George Bush Intercontinental Airport in Houston last week. Travelers waited more than four hours on Tuesday to go through security checkpoints.

item.title: Immigration Slowdown Hits Every Metro Area in the U.S., Census Shows
item.description: Large urban counties and the border were the most affected. And in three-quarters of U.S. counties, population growth either slowed or turned negative.
item.dc:creator: Jeff Adelson and Amy Qin
item.media:credit: IMAGO/Zoonar, via Reuters
item.media:description: The population of Los Angeles County, the country’s most populous, declined by nearly 54,000.

item.title: New York City’s Population Flat After Drop in Immigration
item.description: Newly released census data shows that from June 2024 to July 2025, the number of new residents who were international immigrants dropped by 70 percent compared to a year before.
item.dc:creator: Winnie Hu and Jeff Adelson
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: Many large urban counties saw sharp declines in international migration, though none as substantial as in New York City.

item.title: Melania Trump Appears With a Robot, Saying More Children Should Be Educated by Them
item.description: The first lady, Melania Trump, believes that more children should be educated by “humanoid educators.”
item.dc:creator: Katie Rogers
item.media:credit: Oliver Contreras/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Melania Trump, the first lady, with Figure 3, a humanoid robot developed by Figure AI, an artificial intelligence robotics company.

item.title: Trump to Delay Nominating New C.D.C. Director
item.description: The administration has yet to find a candidate who aligns with Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s agenda while avoiding his unpopular stance on vaccines.
item.dc:creator: Apoorva Mandavilli and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Under federal law, the White House has until midnight on Wednesday to nominate a replacement for former Centers for Disease Control and Prevention director Susan Monarez.

item.title: Kate Marvel, Prominent Climate Scientist, Resigns From NASA
item.description: Kate Marvel, a well-known author, joins an estimated 95,000 people who have left federal science agencies since President Trump returned to the White House.
item.dc:creator: Eric Niiler
item.media:credit: Jackie Molloy for The New York Times
item.media:description: Kate Marvel, a scientist at the NASA Goddard Institute for Space Studies at Columbia University, is one of the almost 100,000 scientists to leave the federal government since President Trump took office.

item.title: Justice Dept. Settles Flynn’s Wrongful Prosecution Suit for $1.25 Million
item.description: The agreement was an extraordinary example of how the Trump administration has offered legal relief to those aligned with the president.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.category: Russian Interference in 2016 US Elections and Ties to Trump Associates
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: Michael T. Flynn, seen in 2018, briefly served as President Trump’s national security adviser in his first term.

item.title: A Phone-Free Childhood? One Irish Village Is Making It Happen.
item.description: Tired of seeing its elementary-school children struggle with online temptations, the town of Greystones proposed a ‘no smart devices’ code. Most everyone bought in.
item.dc:creator: Sally McGrane and Therese Aherne
item.media:description: The seaside town of Greystones, Ireland, is experimenting with a voluntary ban on smart devices for preteen children. Most of the town, from teachers to shopkeepers, is participating in one way or another.

item.title: James Talarico Responds With ‘Love’ To Hegseth Pastor’s Criticism
item.description: James Talarico, the Democratic nominee for Senate in Texas, said, “You may pray for my death, Pastor, but I still love you.” The pastor said he was calling for Mr. Talarico’s religious conversion.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Elizabeth Dias
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: James Talarico at his election night party this month in Austin, Texas.

item.title: With Their Voter Bill Stymied, G.O.P. Leaders Ponder a Plan B
item.description: Republicans are eying a last-ditch procedural maneuver to overcome united Democratic opposition, but the chances for success are slim.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune, the majority leader, brought the SAVE America Act to the floor under intense pressure from President Trump and conservatives who maintained that it was needed to prevent noncitizens from voting,

item.title: What to Know About Democrat Emily Gregory’s Win in Florida
item.description: A mother of three won a statehouse seat that includes Mar-a-Lago, while a union electrician leads in a State Senate race in conservative West Tampa.
item.dc:creator: David W. Chen
item.media:credit: via Emily Gregory for Florida campaign
item.media:description: Emily Gregory, a first-time candidate, won in Palm Beach County, Fla., not by focusing on her most famous constituent but by castigating leaders in Tallahassee, the state capital.

item.title: How U.S.A.I.D. Birth Control Meant for Africa Was Ruined
item.description: The Trump administration had options for offloading contraceptives once destined for Africa, a newly obtained memo shows. Instead, it has let them collect dust and go bad.
item.dc:creator: Jeanna Smialek and Stephanie Nolen
item.media:credit: Hilary Swift for The New York Times
item.media:description: A warehouse in Geel, Belgium, where contraceptives have been stranded since U.S.A.I.D. was defunded and dismantled last year.

item.title: Leon Radvinsky, 43, Dies; Built the Adult-Entertainment Giant OnlyFans
item.description: By leveraging social media and the influencer economy, he turned his website into a byword for online pornography in the 21st century.
item.dc:creator: Clay Risen

item.title: How Bald Reddit Helps People Manage Hair Loss
item.description: A Reddit forum has become a haven for those deciding whether it’s time to shave it all.
item.dc:creator: Stefano Montali
item.media:credit: Michael Swenson for The New York Times

item.title: It Was One of the Cold War’s Greatest Crimes. No One Has Paid a Price.
item.description: No one apart from the Congolese people has ever paid a price for the CIA-backed assassination of Patrice Lumumba.
item.dc:creator: Stuart A. Reid
item.media:credit: Bettmann/Getty Images
item.media:description: Patrice Lumumba in Léopoldville (today Kinshasa, Democratic Republic of Congo) after his arrest in December 1960.

item.title: Zack Polanski on the Rise of Britain’s Green Party
item.description: Zach Polanski of Britain’s left-populist party talks austerity, Labour and the establishment crackup.
item.dc:creator: David Wallace-Wells

item.title: Josh Johnson Ridicules the Army’s Opening to Older Recruits
item.description: “Look, according to People magazine — and apparently the U.S. military — 42 is the new 35,” Johnson said on Tuesday’s “Daily Show.”
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Mamdani Now Plans $1.3 Billion in Cuts in Programs That He Favored
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani is banking on savings from a delay he is seeking in the state mandate for smaller class sizes. He is also scaling back a commitment to expand a rental assistance program.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes and Sally Goldenberg
item.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani faces a $5.7 billion budget deficit that he must fill by July 1.

item.title: Judge Orders Arrest of Matt Bevin, Former Kentucky Governor, for Contempt
item.description: Seven years after leaving office, Matt Bevin, the combative ex-governor, faces charges for not providing financial records sought by his estranged son.
item.dc:creator: Campbell Robertson
item.media:credit: Luke Sharrett for The New York Times
item.media:description: Matt Bevin at his election night party in Louisville, Ky., after losing his re-election bid in 2019.

item.title: Chip Taylor, Writer of ‘Wild Thing’ and Other Classic Rock Hits, Dies at 86
item.description: Mr. Taylor, the younger brother of the actor Jon Voight, found success as a songwriter for the likes of Janis Joplin and Juice Newton.
item.dc:creator: Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Ruth Fremson/The New York Times
item.media:description: Chip Taylor in 2003. “I wasn’t sure about ‘Wild Thing’ at first,” Mr. Taylor recalled in an interview in 1999. “On the demo I told the sound engineer to turn off the lights in the studio, and I just sat on a stool and sang it like a blues guy would.”

item.title: Day Care Worker Stole Millions for Trips and W.W.E. Tickets, U.S. Says
item.description: Murielle Misczak was arrested Wednesday morning and is accused of using the money on wrestling tickets, luxury vacations and food deliveries.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar
item.media:credit: Benjamin Norman for The New York Times
item.media:description: A former day care director pleaded not guilty on Wednesday in Brooklyn federal court to charges she stole millions of dollars from her former employer.
